speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Ella Olson Olson took place at her home in Troy on October 1st, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Pretty rough for the family to get by in the early days. Oh yeah, it was. It was an old schoolhouse that stood that way from our place, but it was close to our place. I started school there when I was five years old, and I didn't understand the English word. We had seats that two kids sat in the same seat.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: No socks, your soap powder had tipped over in your bag. We sat two in each seat.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: And I didn't understand the English word in burnt nail French, did you ever hear him?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh yes. Yeah, well he was the teacher. And so this teacher said something to me there, and so she was supposed to tell me what he said. And she told me that he told me to go up on a stump and the whole recess to keep it going around and around on top of that stump, and I did it. And I didn't know, I had to do what she told me because I couldn't know what he said. And so we had three months of school in the fall and three in the spring. And it was too much snow in the winter to get there, so there was no way to go. Then my dad donated an acre of land on the corner up this way for this new schoolhouse. He got a dollar for it because he had to get a dollar. But he donated that land and they built a nice schoolhouse there. And there's where we went then until they consolidated here in Troy. They had the eighth grade. And there was as many as 44 kids in that school and one teacher in all the grades.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did most of the kids have to learn English in school?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: We had to learn English. And as soon as we stepped out the door, every one of us talked sweet. And there was a family of Germans that lived up there. And those kids learned to talk sweet as good as any of the rest of us. Because that's all they heard. And then the teacher was trying to get us to quit this sweet outside, you know, because we wasn't learning. He wanted us to talk English. And we just wouldn't do it. We just talked sweet. It can differ as to what the teacher said. We were talking sweet outside. And all we learned English was right inside the schoolhouse then what we were learning there. They had all eight grades there. And there was a boy, two boys that came from Sweden. And they were quite old. And they went to school there to learn to talk English. And they stayed with Derek Nelson and that's on this side of Emil Peres' place. So they went to school. And Pleasant Hill was just north from... Well, just north. It was on our land there. So there we all went to school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find that you learned very much in that school with all the kids and what you did?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: I learned everything I knew, I guess.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: We were so bashful. We went to town. We had to go to town to take our eighth grade examinations to the school here. And we were scared to death coming from out there. You know, never hardly see any other people but just ourselves there. And we'd forget everything we knew and we flunked the eighth grade test. And then we had to go another year and take it over again. But I finally made the eighth grade. You forgot because it was so upsetting to come to Troy. Yeah, and we saw all these here town kids, you know, and everything. It was so different that we just forgot everything we knew.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh, we had an awful time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How often did you get to go to Troy to see this, to do things in Hanford?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Well, it was...

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: They'd write down on a slip of paper. And Louie Wallner had a store down here and he had everything in there, groceries and everything. And I'd hand him that slip of paper and I would never even look up. And he'd fill the order and give it to me and out the door I'd go as fast as I could. And we walked. We walked down, but there was... Nora was down there in the corner of that flat. And there was a... at the post office and the grocery store and it was blacksmith shop and the barber shop and the saloon and the saloon keeper's house and that's all that's left there, the saloon keeper's house, but that's remodeled. And we went down there every other day to get the mail. And we lived on top of the hill. And I started to run up on top of that hill and I'd run clear down to the store because it was all downhill, but going back it was all uphill. And I made that trip before time to go to school in the morning. And then when we went to town, we rode with... My dad hauled ties or wood or something and we'd ride on the load to town. And when he went on load, we were supposed to go in the store and get the groceries, whatever they had written down and be ready when he come back because he made two trips a day. And so we made it then that way. And we were so bashful and the whole bunch... all the kids up there were bashful. Couldn't... didn't dare to talk. And then he'd buy a little bit crackers and cheese and that was in place of candy or something. And that's what we... riding home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To eat? Huh? To eat?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: To eat, yeah. We didn't have too much at home. It's just what they raised in the garden, you know, in the spuds. Then they'd kill... butcher an animal in the fall and a pig. So we had plenty of meat and that we had plenty. And so we... we lived on what we had out there.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Did the town kids act like they...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: act like they knew more?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they acted like they were kind of superior?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh yes. Of course, they were home, you know.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: And we were strangers. And we thought, oh, and they were... we thought they were dressed so much nicer and they were so much smarter and they were so much forward, you know, and everything. And they were knew everywhere, everything was. We were just some little green Swedes from the Pleasant Hills. And I'm retaught school up there. But of course those kids... I was out of school by that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: After you finished the eighth grade, what did you do then?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Well I went one year to high school in Moscow and then I thought, oh how foolish to go there. So I went to Spokane and got me a job to work, house work. And then I came home at Christmas time and then in the spring they come and wanted me to go out into the camp to cook. And so we... me and another girl, our neighbor girl, we went out there in the woods up at White Pine. Cooked at the camp, logging camp. And then... then we moved on to the new White Pine, that's the old White Pine. And then we moved on to new White Pine and there was a cook house there for the logging crew and one for the saw mill crew. Because there was a saw mill there. And they had two cooks in the saw mill cook house and then up in the other one we were two girls there. So then that winter went and then in the spring I went to Spokane again and worked. And then I got word that they wanted me to come down here and to go up to the mill and take over. The head cook there. So I went and come down here and then my sister was with me then so we cooked at the saw mill. And then we started in and they had a threshing machine and they had a cook house with the threshing machine then. And it was probably 18, up from 18 to 30 men out to cook. And we went down to Genesee country and on the reservation and we was down there cooking them for the threshing crew. And then it was fall again and then it was go back to the camps. And then the next year was 1920. Now then the war came, 1918. And my husband and I, we was going together for about two years before the war. And then he was gone and went to that and come back and he came back in January 1920. 1919 he came back. So we kept going together and then in 1920, and we was in the cook wagon at the saw mills. And in 1920 I went out in the cook wagon again. And I got in 50 days because it rained. And then the bundles were out in the fields and it rained and by the time they had turned those bundles and got them dry in the sun for three days. Then it was ready to start threshing, then it started raining. And he didn't want to lay us off so he just kept us there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then that cook house, you know what that cook house looked like? You know what a cook house looked like?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: I've seen pictures of some old photos that had a cook house in them. They have little square tables, it was four on each side. I've never seen the inside. It was an aisle in the middle about that wide. And they come in and the cook house stood in the field and it rained and they come in and you can imagine the mud that come in. And we had a shovel there, we had to shovel out that mud before we could do anything, clean the floor, before we could start to work.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh. It was really tight, crowded inside that cook house then. Oh yeah.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: And then at night they'd thresh the dart and then they'd come and eat their supper and we'd clean up the dishes and get things ready for morning. And then we'd have to clean out the floor and get our bedding from under the house and make the bed on the floor. And then about four o 'clock in the morning we'd have to get up and roll that bed up and start in again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much sleep did you get at night?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Well, it could be from nine o 'clock or nine thirty to four. I don't think we needed much sleep the way it looked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that hard work?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: We was washing dishes there and we baked all our bread, all our cookies, and they had lunch in the afternoon. We had lunch and baked everything. And they'd come in right after breakfast and probably say, we're going to move before dinner. And then I'd hurry and set bread because when we was moving it kept shaking, you know, and that bread just raced, you could just see it race. And then we took our peeling, we peeled our spuds and peeled everything that we were going to have. We did that while we were moving. So then when we got to the place, well then it was to hurry and fix it because then it was probably time they wanted to have dinner pretty quick. And it was just a rush. And then in the afternoon we had to have fresh cake or something baked and have coffee and send out to them in the afternoon. And then it was to have supper at night, so it was hard work. I don't see how we could do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it rushing all the time?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Same thing day after day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have time to sit down, relax, drink some coffee?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Not much.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: We didn't have much time. It was just to keep on going. That was at 19, 20. And they didn't finish trashing because it was mud and rain they couldn't finish.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When it rained did they just leave the cookhouse where it was and everybody stayed there?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: So he just kept us and paid us and we were there cooking for the ones that stayed there. And I don't see how we could do it myself. Now I couldn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you serve all the men in one shift?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: No, they'd come. And probably these four at this table would probably be done and then they'd go. Well then we'd set out clean plates and stuff for this and other ones that come in a little later. And then it got so that we had to feed the ones that hauled the grain to town. And we wasn't supposed to do that but they fixed it so that we did. And those came anytime. But we had to see that we had something for them to eat. Oh that, it was hard. Well so then in 1920s when I got married, after this harvest, 13th of November, and they finished trashing there, they sent us home in October. And then they finished trashing in November because it straightened up pretty good and the grain was black. The shocks and everything was black. But they finished it and then after that harvest we got married the 13th of November.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did your husband do at that time?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Was he working in the... He was working in the woods and the camps and the logging and all that time. We went together for about four years, one year, five years. We didn't have time to get married, it was too much work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really? Is that why it took so long to get married? Because it was too much work?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Yeah, I didn't have time. I wanted to get some more of that cooking done and some more money and then we only got a dollar a day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For the cooking?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: And that last year when we had all those 50 days we got a dollar and a half. Did you get to save all the money that you earned or did you have to... We sent it home, that's how that barn was built. We gave their... Dad had to have a new barn and so we cooked them, worked my sister and I then and sent the money up and paid for all the stuff that they built the barn out. So we didn't get any of it. That doesn't sound quite fair to me. No, but that's the way it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you feel about it then? As a girl did it bother you that all the money was going?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: It was a fear that we should have that barn paid for and that we wanted to have that done, help them to get that barn paid. It didn't bother us, we was just going to work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many kids in the family there?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: We were nine kids, now it's the oldest.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh, that's right.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: And there's only three of us left. All five boys are dead, one sister.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your mother have to work very hard?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh yeah, she went out to work when she was real young and they even sent her to school in Moscow. She worked for those places, housework there. And she was real young, she was only nineteen when she got married and then she had worked out for many years. And with her, now they had to walk clear from this country here up to Moscow, there was no Troy here. And they walked up, walked to Moscow and her dad would be there the day that he knew that she had a payday and get the cheque. And they were a big family and they were homesteading up in the country here. And so, I call it hard times, but then when depression came I thought that was worse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When your mother was, this working she was doing, this was after she stopped going to school in Moscow?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: She didn't go to school only those people where she worked, she didn't get any schooling.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: But they sent her to school so that she'd learn a little and then she'd work her way through that way. And she didn't have much schooling. And my dad, I don't know if he had any schooling at all. My husband in Minnesota, he was born in Minnesota, and there they had, instead of the fourth grade or something, they had fourth reader. And he went as far as the fourth reader. And he thought then after he'd seen how our kids was, he thought that was put near as far as the seventh and eighth grade. But they called her fourth reader, and that's as far as he was.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: So they didn't have much schooling.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well for raising a family so big, did that mean that your mother would have to spend all her time taking care of the kids in the house or did she do other work too?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh she had to do outside work, she had to be there and help with the grain and the shopping and hay and stacking hay, grain.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: She had to work outside and help. And I know when we, we heralded, he plowed it, and then I had to herald afterwards. And I walked behind that herald until I had great big blisters on my feet. Must be a doubt. Okay.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: You didn't, didn't you have a jacket at all?

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: No. Cold weather doesn't bother me. It might rain. I'm not too sensitive against this weather. Okay, bye. Goodbye.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: So, so, so you, you followed behind with the...

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: I did, and then in the spring, in the summer, when it was ripe, but then he'd cut it and then we had, I had to rake it. See, it was me and then it was, we were two more girls. And then they got a boy after that, so I was the only one that could help with anything.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: So you did some pretty heavy work yourself then as you were growing up. Yeah, I worked, I can say I worked like a man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it, was it hard, would it, as a girl, did it exhaust you doing work like that? Did it tire you out?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh yeah, we was pretty tired, but we still wanted to go out on Saturday night. And if we didn't have everything done like it was supposed to be, we couldn't go out. And we worked so much harder so that we'd have everything done so we could go out on Saturday night. And we used to have dances then in the Placent Hill Schoolhouse or the White Pine Schoolhouse, and that's where we wanted to go. So we were still able to go out and dance on Saturday night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the dances like then?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh, we danced polkas and square dances and shoutishes and waltzes and two -steps.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go there with your fiancé?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: No, he wasn't around yet then, this is before. We just walked up there and had a good time with everybody.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just local kids? Were the parents there?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Well, they come in from other places, it was packed full there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the older folks there too, the parents there too?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh, they could go if they wanted to, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they serve a lunch too?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just the dancing?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: No, we just danced till morning.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Till morning? Till Sunday morning?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Yeah, and our wedding, I was married in our house, in that house there. And after the wedding we had asked if we could go to the schoolhouse to have the dancing and the party. And they said we could. And they cooked coffee in the boiler. And they just kept drinking coffee all night long, those I stayed in the house. And then we went up there to the schoolhouse and it was five o 'clock in the morning when we left, got done. They got done to go home. And then we went home and changed our clothes and we had promised that we would scrub and clean that schoolhouse that was ready for school on Monday morning. And this was five o 'clock Sunday morning, we had to get water hot and get a hold up there and clean that schoolhouse.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: So, nobody could do what we did now in this time and age.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you and your husband went up there just to see you or did your friends go through it?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: No, some of the kids from home went with us and helped us move benches and stuff. It was just from the house there. Because then everybody had gone home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So that was for your wedding then?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: That was our wedding.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had an all night dance afterwards?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Yes, we had an all night dance. Well that sounds great. And the day after two, we'd be all scrubbing.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Cleaning. Now, I've got to ask you about these dances you went to up there on a Saturday night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you usually go until Sunday morning? It was often they would go say all night long?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh yeah, they went all night long. That sounds great, but I'm surprised people could take it and then have anything left that they could do on Sunday. And the trouble was, White Pine Schoolhouse was quite a ways off.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: And when I cooked down at White Pine Mill, that was pretty far to walk. But we walked over to that schoolhouse and we just got back up to the camp in time to fix breakfast.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: We had a lot of fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about, did many people go to church on Sunday?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Yes, we went to church and Sunday school every Sunday.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: After staying up all night the night before?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh yeah, we went.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: We went and then we didn't go to bed. We didn't go to bed Sunday. We didn't go to bed until Sunday evening when we went to bed. We didn't go and sleep like they do now. Because I know if they're up now until 12 or 1 o 'clock, well they have to sleep the whole next day. Pretty near half a day, I know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go to the church at Nora?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Yeah, and that is what is the Lions Hall here now. It was the Nora Church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was the, what was the name of the church?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: It was a Congregational, the Swedish Mission Church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you have any idea what happened to the church records?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: No, I don't have any idea. But the church is this Lions Hall. And that church was, I was born in 1897, on the 18th of November. And the first meeting in that church was on Thanksgiving, just one week. I was born on a Thursday and the next Thursday was Thanksgiving. And that was the first meeting held in that church. It was that new then. So it was built in 1897. And then they sold it. They could never leave anything standstill. The schoolhouse was torn down, the church was sold here to be the Lions Hall. But it's still alive anyway, so it's a church, part of it there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember the church services as being like?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: All Swede. It's all in Swedish.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Sunday school and the preaching and everything was Swedish.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of services would the preacher preach? What kind of services?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Well, it was in the Bible.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Same thing as in the English Bible, but it was in Sweden.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: You know, he preached.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And Sunday school?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: He preached. We had Sunday school at 10 o 'clock and then he preached at the meeting at 11. And then in the evening they had meeting. And I went to confirmation. I was confirmed in that church. Then we went to confirmation on Saturday before noon. Studied then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who taught the Sunday school?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh, it was Sankwist and Emil Pearsons was married to a Sankwist girl. And Alfred Johnson and Oliboman and all those were teachers.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: All in Swedish.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: It was a lot of fun, though.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: That's quite a while ago. I heard that the old timers, those from the old country, that the men were used to telling the women what to do and they were used to obeying. Well, that's still further back. That's before my time. And it wasn't that way after... When my dad and mother was married. That's way before that.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: So with your parents, they sort of shared...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They made decisions together?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Yeah. So they...

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: More like it is now. But for a long time before their time was this way, the men was the head.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who told the kids what to do? Your mother or father?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Well, my mother told us and did what she could and if we didn't do what that was, she tattled to dad and then we got a good lickin'. So we thought we better do what she tells us or she'll tackle to tell it. He wouldn't spare the rod.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he treat the girls differently than the boys? Was it easier on the girls?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Well, I don't know. Of course, they had three girls first and then they got two boys. And of course, then when they were so tickled to have those boys, that they got more spoiled than those girls did. And made over and then us girls was that much older then that we... We could work and do some things where those boys were...

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Just pets. Pests.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: As I know my oldest brother, whenever they went to town, I had a rope ready. And we had a stove that had legs on it.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Square stove.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: It had legs on it. And when they left, and as soon as they went to town and I was home there with the kids, I took that rope and I tied him. And I tied him to that leg of the stove there. He was tied till I seen him come home. And then I let him loose. Cause he was so mean. He hit us and he... I just couldn't have him loose. One time I didn't get him and it was a pantry. That little window on the side there. That was a pantry. And we got in there and we locked ourselves in. And we didn't dare to go out cause he had an axe and he was gonna kill us. He had a butcher knife and everything. So we was locked in that pantry till they come home. And so I just learned that I'm gonna find a rope and I did find a rope. And I hid that rope and just kept it and nobody knew where that rope was but me. And as soon as they left, they wasn't worn out by the gate till I had him and tied him. And I had him tied to that stove till they got home. You did that more than once. Oh, all the time. Every time they went. Wherever they went, I did that because we couldn't let him go loose. They let you do it. They didn't know it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't he tell?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: No sir, we told him. If he does, we're gonna just beat him to death. He never told. Didn't dare to do that because I could handle him pretty good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was he like that, do you think?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: I don't know. He was just gonna show that he could take over and be boss.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Run the house?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: He wasn't gonna take orders from any of us girls. He was gonna run it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much older were you than he?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Five years. And I was seven years older than our next brother. So they was the two boys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did he start getting over that?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Well, I don't know. He was quite big. He was in school. And got over it more and more. So he got better. But this was when he was really unrated. Four or five years old. Just awful.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Spoiled?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Yeah, they spoiled him because they had the boys. And then we had three girls, you know, and when they got a boy, they were pretty tickled. He got too spoiled. And they liked, they often liked to have boys. Yeah, they wanted boys. They wanted boys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was that?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: I don't know. I heard because they worked in the fields better and they worked harder on farms. Probably they thought it was cheaper to raise them and I don't know what. I don't know that it would have been cheaper. But there was five boys and we were four girls. And one girl died and now all five boys are dead.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: This one that was so ornery, when him and his wife went out fishing up past Bovill and they had a camper. They lived in Lewiston. And they had a camper and they went up there and got there in the afternoon and they had drank some coffee in their camper. And went out and got the limb with the fish and then he said he wasn't feeling very good. So he said, I think we better probably go home. And when they got up to the camper, he said, you'll have to drive. He said, I'm getting worse. He said, I'm getting awful sick. And so they got ready then to turn around.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Before they got into Bovill, he said, I feel much better now. And he says, but we'll keep on going home. And then in Bovill, when she got into Bovill, she stopped and then he was dead. So it went that fast.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, because you were the oldest girl, did you help your mother a lot in raising the other kids?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Yeah, we had to have a tub and a washboard and wash the clothes that way and bake bread.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: I don't think I was more than five years old when I started to bake the bread.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: And when they went to town, they left me there with the kids and they'd show me on the clock when that little hand would come over to that. They'd be gone eight hours. And when the little hand got on that number there, then they'd be coming home. And I'd clean the house and I'd do all I could. And we'd go down the canyon, it was a little canyon below there, and we'd play in the playhouse. And we'd watch that clock because we knew they were going to come home then. So we had everything in order when they got home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, for raising you and teaching you what to do and what not to do, how did they do that? Did they tell you how you should behave?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: They said we couldn't go no place unless we behaved. And they said they don't know what we do. We behaved pretty good. Well, all the kids up there were good. There wasn't any bad kids. There was no dope and no drinking or nothing. It was just to have a good time, that was all. Now, if they'd have a dance at that schoolhouse now, if that schoolhouse was there, I bet there wouldn't be one kid up there, young people there. They wouldn't go to a place like that. There was, we didn't have no cars just to go. And if the boys from town come out, they had livery barns here, they had two livery barns here. They had horses, they could rent the horses and the buggy. They'd come and take us out. And then as far as we could go, it was up to Deary. About as far as they could ride.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about being honest? Did they put a lot of stress on that, your parents, the kids to be honest?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh yeah. We had to be honest.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: We had to know everything. We couldn't cheat anything. We couldn't swipe a penny or anything because they'd know just exactly what we should have. What we should bring back from the store or something. We were made honest. So we were all brought up to be honest.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was all this work hard on some of the women or some of the girls?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh it must have been. It must have been awful hard to be parents in them days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it was hard on your mother to do?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: I think, well it must have been. She was little. I don't think she ever weighed over 97 pounds.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: A real small woman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then she did all that work?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Yeah. You can see it. She's on the picture there. She's just a little woman. She lived to be almost 99. My dad was only 54 when he died. I think what he died was from cancer and they didn't know what it was. They didn't know what anything was in them days that long ago. He was paralyzed clear from here down to his legs. They couldn't even touch the bed. He hollered. So he died, I think he had cancer. What I think was it started with this prostrate gland and they didn't know it. Doctors didn't know it. And then it turned to cancer. That's what I think he died from. Because they never said what he died from. Doctors didn't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you went up to Spokane, is that where you heard there was the best place to work for a young woman to get a job? Was that why you went up there?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Well, we heard that there was housework and people wanted girls to work. And when we went to Spokane, it was the neighbor girl across the road and I went. And we'd never been to Spokane. We'd never been to any big town. And then it was an Adventist minister that lived out there. And he, his wife, knew a girl in Spokane. She lived in the basement of the Adventist church in Spokane. And she knew her and she said she's going to write to that girl and have her carry a magazine and meet us at the depot. And we were supposed to carry the same magazine. So when we got off the train, we had that magazine and she met us and we went with her home. And then we got a hold of a newspaper and read in there where we should go and look for work. And we went and found those places. She helped us so we could get, know what they were to go.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: So we got work and we worked clear to Christmas.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Tell me, who did you work, what kind of people did you work for? Were they people who were pretty well to do?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: And they always told us we should, when we go to Spokane to work, we should always go where there was a lot of kids in the family. Because then if it was an old couple or just a couple, you know, they'd expect more from the girls that worked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But if there's kids there, you know how kids can drag out things all the time?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: So in the place where I got was four kids and the one that the other girl got was three kids. And we made it fine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was it better to have, what would the kids do? The kids would help you work?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: No, if we didn't make everything perfect, well then they could, the kids would drag out stuff, you know how kids are. So we got by with a lot of, otherwise if they'd have been just a couple or that, we'd have expected everything to be just perfect, you know. So we got along just fine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they, did the people still expect a lot of you, the people you worked for?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Well, I don't know, we kept their house clean and cooked for them and washed the dishes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they rich?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh yeah, they were well to do people. And they took care of their own kids. They had a woman that took care of kids, a nurse I guess they called them. I didn't know what it was then, but I guess that's what they called them, somebody to take care of the kids. And they sent all the washing out, so I didn't have to do any washing. It was to, and she'd tell me what to fix for the meals, you know. And so it wasn't too bad. And then we'd have one afternoon off and then Sunday afternoon off and one afternoon during the week to go to town. So we got out. And then we could go out after nine o 'clock in the evening, we would get through with all our work at nine. And then if we wanted to go to a dance or something downtown we could go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this friend of yours nearby?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Well we met, we met downtown on Riverside. And she'd take a different bus and I'd have a different bus, but we'd meet there somewhere. Whoever got there first just waited until the other bus come and we...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where would you go to dance?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh we went to Odin Hall, that's a Swedish Hall.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the name?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Odin, O -D -I -N Hall. And that was a Swedish Hall and we went there. So that's where we went to the dances.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they treat you very well, the people at the house? They didn't treat you like just a maid?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: No, they were real nice.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get to eat with the family?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Did you eat... No, they ate in the dining room, but I wouldn't have wanted to eat in there anyway. But they ate in the dining room and I'd eat in the kitchen.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: So I learned to cook and then I got the job to cook at the camps and in the meals.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You didn't know how to cook before you went up there?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh I knew, yeah, I learned at home. So I knew how to, so I could get along alright. But I had all my cookbooks with me and I ran them and cooked, cooked, whatever they wanted and I fixed it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of the city of Spokane compared to little country like, country places like here?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: We thought it was so big that we didn't, oh my, and Spokane wasn't very big then as it is now. We thought it was so big and oh my, we was really hot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you meet your husband?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Well, up at the camps.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were cooking?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Yeah, he was at Old White Pine and the boys kind of come and hung around the cookhouse a little bit. They come in and dry dishes of course. And one thing, another one, sometime, another one, another time. And you can always take a better liking to one than you can to another, you know. And then he'd come in there and he'd get the dish towel and dry the dishes and he'd come in and sit around and that. And pretty soon, he had another girl up at Dearie when, first, so this man that run the camp and this girl that I've been with all the time across the road there, we were cooking there. And he was kind of going with her. And so he asked me if I wanted, he was going to, one of the other boys in the camp there to go with and we were going to go down to the Nora Church. And he was going to drive with the hack and the horses down to Nora Church. And he wanted to know if I wanted to know. I said I didn't want any of them. And when we got on the road. He wanted to know if I wanted one of the other boys to go with. And I said no, I didn't want any of them other boys to go. And my husband was already, he had a girlfriend in Dearie, so he was up there, he'd go up there. And when we was driving from the camp up to there, we met him on the road.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So this, Joe asked me, he said should we pick him up?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: And I said yeah. So we picked him up and he went with us to church and then he quit going to Dearie and he started coming to see me I guess. So that's the way it started.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it just, what happened when she met him on the road?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Yeah, we just met, he'd come walking, he'd been to Dearie and he'd come walking back to the camp. And we were going, driving out and we met him there. And then he went to church. Yeah, and then he got in and we went to church. So that's how it started, kind of when we went together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think you kind of liked each other from when you first met?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: I guess so. Tell me what it was like in the camp. I've heard some about the cooking in Threshing Cruise before, but I've never heard about cooking for the, in these lumber camps.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh, it was just like in the house. Well in the camp, they had a dining room and a kitchen. And the dining room had a long table and we'd set them tables and we'd fix the meals, fix roasts and whatever meat we got ahold of. They butchered right up there and hung it up and we'd have to go out there and wag it off ourselves. And I got scars all over where I chopped the knife into my hand. I couldn't have chopped that finger off one time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just cutting slabs off the meat?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Yeah, because I'd miss, the meat was frozen part of the time. And they'd slip and it'd get me like, oh I was all burnt up. I got scars all over from that. And we'd wag off if they wanted steak, if we were going to have steak, well it was to cut off so we could get steak. Water the roast, we'd have to go out there and saw off and get some roasts. And so we, and then we'd just cook great big kettles of spuds and vegetables and we'd bake bread and everything went, just kept on going.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the conditions like there for the, you know, as a place to live and work?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: I've heard that some of the logging camps were pretty rough in those days as far as, you know. These were all just local people and it wasn't any rough.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: It was just like being home and everybody was clean and they cleaned the bronc houses and everything was clean. And we'd scrub the kitchen floor, the dining room floor and the kitchen floor and it was clean.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: I don't know, I don't remember that we were ever awfully tired.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So the work wasn't too hard?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: It couldn't have been or we were terrible strong, I don't know. We just kept on going.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there men around those, some men around there that the girls didn't trust?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: No, I don't know. I trusted everybody that was there. I was there alone down at Standard Mill. I was alone a whole summer with a bunch of them there. And in the evening, well they'd sit down by the mill and play cards. And I'd go down there and I'd sit there with them. And then one time I got up and I stepped on a roller and started going. And I was just ready to roll under the mill when one of the boys caught my leg and I didn't go down. Oh, I didn't know that thing was going to go when I stepped on it. And another time I went out on the pond. Those boys walked on them logs and they never moved. And I thought, well I don't see any reason why I can't do it. So one Sunday morning I went out there on the pond and I was going to walk on those logs like they did. And the logs started going, you know, and you jump on another one and that'll keep it going. And they come running from the bunkhouse there and got me out of that mess. But I thought I could walk them logs just as good as any of those could. But they laid still for them but when I got on them they started rolling. I don't know. I should have balanced them some way I guess and didn't know it. Well I had heard that, for instance, like at the mill in Nora there was quite a bit of drinking that the men did in the early days there. The lumberjack, the guys that were loggers there. Yeah, but if they did, they went out on Saturday night to town and if they did they drank when they were out but they didn't have any drinking there. So I was there where I was. And I was alone with those, oh about six men down there in standard mill one whole summer. And I wasn't scared of any of them. I trusted all of them. They were just real good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many, did you and a friend cook at the, say how many cooks for the crew at the White Pine saw mill there?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh that was about seventeen I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Seventeen men and what?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: And then the logging crew, well they must have been ten or twelve.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And how many cooks?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Well there were two cooks for the saw mill and two for the logging camp.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: So it was work but poor pay. But everything was cheap then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At that time all the money that you were making in Spokane and here too went to your folks?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Not what I made in Spokane. I spent there for clothes and stuff there. They didn't get any of that. But we worked for the mills and the camps there. We sent that all home to pay for them. We bought a piano. That was the beginning. We sent in the catalog for a piano and then we had to pay that. And then they built the barn and then we started paying on the barn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you use the piano a lot?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Well I didn't. The kids at home did. The boys played. My brothers could pick up any instrument there was to play on. And they could play on the comb. They could play the malt harp and the comb and the accordion and guitar and violin and piano. Anything. They just sit down and just play. And there isn't one of the grandchildren. Well there's one. One of the grandchildren. That's the younger kids. Their kids. That is that way. And he plays anything. He can just pick up anything and just play anything.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: You know you said that in Moscow you went to school there for a year and then decided.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think there? Why wasn't it worth your while to go to school?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Well I worked for my room and board.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: And I had to work and then I had to do my studying because I had to know my work from school. And I just thought that I'm not going to go there no more. I just couldn't see that it would pay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why wasn't it worth it? Because it was just too much work for you to get out of it?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Well I had to work for room and board and that was a lot of work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you work for a well to do family there?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Well no. Not too well. But they didn't have any kids. It was just him and her there. But I had to do all the housework after I got home from school and work with Saturdays and Sundays. And clean the house and cook for them. And then go to school. I had to do my studying sometime and I just couldn't see any sense in that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you have gone to high school in Troy if you wanted to then?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Well my folks didn't want me here. They sent me up to Moscow to work for my room and board to get to go. Because they didn't have any money to pay for it. And this way I worked myself too the first year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far, where you lived in Nora was about five miles from town here?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Yeah. That was too far to go every day. Oh yeah it was pretty far and there was no buses, no cars, no nothing.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: A lot of snow.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: So that must have made it rough for all the kids to go to school. Yeah there wasn't any of the kids up there that went to school. They couldn't go to any higher school than what they got there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well when you were thinking about getting married then, when you did get married, did you have to have very much to get married? Did you feel you needed to have much in the way of clothes and dishes and all that stuff?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Well no, we had a big wedding and we got an awful lot of stuff. And we went to sales and bought the furniture and we got that chair, there was a wedding present.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And we got married in 1920 and that's how long ago, 56 years ago isn't it?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Yeah that chair we got from Hotel Thompson, he had the hotel down here. He gave us that chair. So that still is a wedding present and we used it. And when my kids, we had two boys, and when they were small I'd put a pillow in there and I'd put a chunk of wood under the runner there. And that's where they slept in the, I didn't have any baby buggy or anything for them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They slept where?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: They slept there in that chair. And it's in good shape yet.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: It looks like.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So where did you live, in Troy here?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: No, we bought a 40 acre farm east, between Deary and Troy. And there's where both our kids are born. And then he got work here at the warehouse, at the Green's warehouse then. And then we moved to town. And then we lived in town the rest of our days. He worked at Green's and then he got to be manager of this late accounted grain growers. So he was manager there and when he was 65 he quit and got social security. And then he'd work, Rauch just had a saw mill up here and he'd pile lumber for him on the top of the hill there. And he'd work with the rock crusher and just a little bit here and there, you know, just a little.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So was he stationed in Troy for the grain growers?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Yeah, he was right here in Troy at the grain growers at the warehouse there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what did you do after marriage then?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Well, we moved to town here and then that house across the street from that hotel, that was a hospital. We had a doctor there. I got work there, so I cleaned and I washed there. And that was a real hospital. He operated there and everything, that doctor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was that?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Dr. Myers was his name. And then I started to get in on this pea picking in Moscow. And all those women here in Troy almost went up there and picked peas, sorting peas.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You went into Moscow?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Yeah, we worked up there. We worked at Washburn and Wilson and we worked at Pure Lion and at Sabs and then we picked beans down here at the warehouse. Then it's a belt that comes towards you and the peas come rolling and you have to pick out all the mixtures and say that these are seed peas they want. And if there's any round peas in there, we had to get all those round peas out of there. They didn't want any mixtures in it. So we worked there, oh I worked there for many years. Until 65, 64.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you start working there? After the Depression?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh yeah. And then they cut mica down here. We got mica cutting down here in between the peaking peas.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where? Down here?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Down in that school bus garage is where we cut mica.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it pea picking? Was that work very tedious, having to look at the same things over time?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: We had to drink coffee so we should be able to stay awake.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: In the winter we were on the ship from 3 in the afternoon to 8 hours. 9, 11. Because we got time off for our supper. And it was 40 below zero. And the car was frozen and we were just going to go home. And when we got out by the cemetery, Moscow Cemetery, the car stopped. And it found out that they had stolen the gas out of it. So we didn't have any gas. So we tried to make people stop and nobody wanted to stop because you know how cold it was. And there we stood. And so then one boy, it was a boy and a girl, and they were going to Genesee and he stopped. And he took the lady that drove into Moscow and got them to bring us some gas. And the next day when we come to work we could see where they had taken the gas out of the car because it was on the ground. That snow, ice had melted on the ground where the gas had come down on it and they had stolen our gas. And it was cold. Boy, it was cold. It just hit and popped in trees and everything else. But we kept on all winter until spring and next year we went back again. But then we got on in the morning. So we worked from morning until we got off at 3 o 'clock.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Before you were working evening shift, when was that from? It started in the evening?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: It started in the afternoon and then 8 hours when we did that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: 11 o 'clock, wasn't it?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Boy, so then you didn't get home until...

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh, now midnight.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: And then we had to do our work at home too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you worked out quite a bit then when you were married?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: I didn't have the time as long as I was able to work. Until I... I took my Social Security when I was 62. I didn't get as much as... as if I'd have waited until 65. But I took it. And after that I hadn't worked much at anything else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Back before the Depression though, in the 20s, the work that you did was all at the hospital?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Yeah, and then... and then we got food from... they had food so we could get beans and different kinds of canned meats and everything just to go downtown and get it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: During the Depression?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Yeah. And so we got food there and then they put in the sewer here in town. And they got so many hours to work on that so each person would get in some time. And they paid them for that. We paid... we were renting a house...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: where is it?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Over that way. And we paid... the rent was $6 a month and we couldn't pay it. And it was a bachelor... he's a rich bachelor that we rented from... and all he asked... well, he rented $6 a month and we couldn't pay him. And he says, well let her go to the facility later. And then he died. And we still owned for about three months of that rent and he died. And... $6 a month we couldn't pay it.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Did your husband... was your husband...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: were your husband and you both out of work when the Depression hit?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Yeah. And he couldn't get work.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: And there was no work. So that's how I got in at the hospital there to work there. Oh, it was hard. It wasn't anything. And the kids wanted this and the kids wanted that and they couldn't have it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it worse, you think, than it was when your parents were raising the family?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: I think that Depression was worse.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: I think so.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Did you have to worry about having enough food to eat before they brought in these...

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: No. We got enough because we'd get plenty from there and then I'd get a hold of some and canned some, you know, and something in the summer so that we'd have some canned vegetables and fruit and stuff.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: And...

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: but we got an awful lot. We got big sacks of beans and peas and stuff to cook, you know. So we got lots to eat from that relief.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Before that relief started there was a time there wasn't any relief, wasn't there in the Depression?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Yeah, it was pretty hard then.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: But that started in pretty soon after, though it wasn't too long until we got this relief food. And then I worked until I quit. 62. I'll be 79 next month now.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh, next year. That'll be 80 next year.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh, that's not that old.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh, that sounds awful. I know lots of people in their 80s and 90s. Oh, yeah. Yeah, but there's much future ahead for anybody getting old.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Just your life to live it as it is from day to day. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Then when he died in 1973, and then we lived, I lived up in the house there for two years then, and then I thought it was getting too much yard work and too big a house to keep. So I sold it. I sold my house. I got $15 ,000 for it. And when we bought it we paid $3 ,100 for it. I sold for $15 ,000. Then I bought our groceries and that. And then we got some money, we paid on it, and it went on until we could pay it out. And so they had a lot of patience with the people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The bank did?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: The stores. Oh, the bank, we didn't have anything, we didn't have anything much to do with the bank.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: I got a book here that shows what maybe you have already seen it. I had surgery on my knees. I didn't have a statement of a cent.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh, yes. I'm sure it was like that in the 1920s when you were here. It's there. It's a picture of it there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But I mean, what do you remember the town as being like? Had it changed much from the pioneer days when you were a kid?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh, yeah, it sure has because they were wooden buildings and that's all brick and mortar. I don't mean between then and now, but I mean between then and say like the 20s.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you moved here to live, was it very different than it had been when you were a child?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: No, it wasn't much different because it was wooden buildings and the bank was where the post office is now. So it was just that little bank and the post office was at the other end down there by the hotel there, some place on this side of the hotel. And it was, one big store that they had is the school bus garage. That was one big store. Williamson had that store. And he had furniture and clothes and groceries and everything you wanted in there. And then he quit that and just had grocery stores here. Now we have only one grocery store left.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you do much of your shopping there at Williamson's during that time?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Yeah, we bought a lot of stuff there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find that when you were a kid growing up that your parents made do without a lot of things?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh yeah, we all had to do without.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: We couldn't have this or that or anything. I don't think any of the kids in that day and age could. They were all poor, just settling there. There was woods cleared up to your door. There wasn't hardly any fields or anything there when my dad moved in there and got that place. And it was just starting, breaking up land, cutting wood, selling wood.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For you in the time between when you were first married and then the depression, did you just raise a family then or did you work out then?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: And the kids were small so I stayed home. I was home then. We just had two boys. So then I didn't go to work until I started at the hospital down there. I worked all the time after that. We started picking peas and cutting mica and everything. Cutting mica, that's hard. That's awful hard. And it's dust. Mica dust. Fine, real fine stuff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How do you cut it? What did you cut it with?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: We had a sharp knife and we had to cut it so it slanted and peel it off so it looked nice and smooth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that what was being mined near Deary?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Yeah, up in mica mine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But when you went to work, did that make a big difference to the family income?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Well I guess so because we got the house paid for in a hurry and so I put my money into the house and then we had to... so it helped. Helped a lot.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: You said the kids wanted things that they couldn't have in the depression. I was just wondering what kind of things they wanted. Oh they wanted a bicycle and they wanted things to play with and different things that some of the other kids had. So they couldn't have it.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: But we lived through the depression and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the community in town here was as close as it was out where you grew up by Nora? The neighborhood?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh I don't know. There it was just like one big family. All the neighbor kids played together more that way. But I don't know. I've always liked it here. I've always been here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you think maybe it wasn't that people weren't as close here?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Now nobody has time to go visit anybody else. But I mean back in the 20s and the 30s and in those days. No I don't think there was... it was like being up there in the country. And then they got cars and started going in cars. And nobody stayed home anymore. They didn't go together like they did before the cars came. Then we couldn't get no other place but up until Schoolhouse. Everybody went in the same wagon. Yeah we went where we could go to.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Together.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Together. Well yeah we went up there and we met there and stayed there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about in town here did you take part in any of the groups that were going on in town and the activities they had?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh I did belong to the Rebekah once. My husband belonged to the Odd Fellows. And that's all that I ever joined or was into. Then they died. Both of them died out so they go to Moscow now those that wanted to go on. And then we go to church. We went to the church. That was all.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Well I think I've stayed long enough I should get going.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: You didn't care for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that why she quit then?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: I wonder. I think it might have been part of why.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Depression, things have changed really well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you used to have friends, women friends that you spent much time with that you were pretty close to?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Well we didn't have time because we were working all the time and we were together then and visiting all the time when we was riding. Whenever we was eating our lunch and everything. So we didn't have much time for two. When we were home we had to work at home to get caught up at our own house. So we never went visiting very much. And now nobody has time to visit. Nobody comes and nobody goes. And now I've got, I can't go up a hill so now I'm stuck. Because it's wherever you go you go up here. You see they operated on my knee and put in a piece of steel here and a piece of steel there and took this cap off and scraped it. And here's two drains here and that's a year ago. Yeah that was a year ago in August. So that gets pretty stiff. Yeah I would bet. Depression would come now, I don't know what in the world these kids would do. Because they used to get in whatever they want to and big wages and work wherever they go. I don't know what they'd do if depression came now for these kids. All of them have cars and whatever, everything. Boats and cars and even airplanes. I don't know what they'd do if depression would come now. They couldn't take it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think there could be another depression?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: It's hard to tell what it's going to be.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Never know.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: It's hard to tell what it'll be.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: I was going to ask you one thing about working.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you enjoy the working that you did or would you rather not have had to work if you had a choice?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Oh no, we wanted to work.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: I wanted to work.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: I don't want to work now anymore. I couldn't get a job or any work if I did try for some tool.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: But that work that you did, it sounds like it was pretty hard work. Well it was all hard work.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: That cutting, that starting piece there, that was awful hard. You'd sit there and your back would just about break. And that's, we all got backaches from it. It was all hard work. There wasn't any easy work. I got easy work now though with this house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the pay any good for that? For the sorting piece?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: No, the sorting piece. Oh yeah, we got a dollar and a half an hour.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that pretty good?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: It was thirty -five cents an hour when we started. Then they raised the wages off and on until we got a dollar and a half. That was good. But we started in with thirty -five cents an hour. But first we started picking peas.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that in the thirties after the depression was over?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: After, yeah.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: In eight hours, eight times thirty -five.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: That wasn't very much in a day, was it?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That would be two dollars and eighty cents.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: Then they raised it up to forty cents and then up to fifty and then it kept going up. I don't know what they paid, they must pay quite a bit now. Because they're still picking peas. It went up. And then we worked on Saturday, we get time and a half for that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you work eight hours then on Saturday? Did that mean that you and your husband didn't have much time together?

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: We didn't have any time together then because it was just working. And Sunday I had to clean up the house and get ready to go work again on Monday. We didn't have time for nothing. It was just to work to try and get ahead. So we bought the house and then we had to work to pay that. And I made pretty good to pay for that. We paid only three thousand and one hundred. That's not much for a house in this time and age. It was quite a bit then.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: We lived in that house for thirty -five years. And I just sold it then because I just couldn't take care of it anymore. And the big yard had flowers all around the yard and trees all over.

speaker: Ella Olson Olson
words: I know that can get pretty...
